specification.topic-statement: The database message insertion system validates message kinds and persists conversation messages to a database session.

scope.in-scope: Kind string validation against the set of recognized message kinds
Parameterized insertion of a message record into a database session
In-memory construction of a tool result message record
scope.boundary: Reading or querying previously inserted messages is not covered here.
Creating or managing database sessions is out of scope.

field.name: id
field.notes: 0 when not yet persisted; assigned by database on insert

field.name: kind
field.notes: one of the 13 recognized kind strings

field.name: session_id
field.notes: positive integer identifying the database session

field.name: tool_call_id
field.notes: ID of the tool call being responded to

field.name: name
field.notes: name of the tool that was invoked

field.name: output
field.notes: raw output text from the tool

field.name: success
field.notes: extracted from the tool_success field of the output JSON; defaults to false if parsing fails

behavior.kind-validation: Exactly 13 kind strings are recognized: "clear", "system", "user", "assistant", "tool_call", "tool_result", "mark", "rewind", "agent_killed", "command", "fork", "usage", and "interrupted". Validation is an exact, case-sensitive string match against each member of this set. Any string not in the set causes validation to return false; any exact match returns true.
behavior.insertion-preconditions: Before inserting a message, the system asserts that the database handle and its underlying connection are both non-null, and that the session identifier is a positive integer greater than zero. Violations of these preconditions abort the process immediately rather than returning an error.
behavior.insertion-execution: A temporary memory context is created for the duration of the insertion. A parameterized INSERT statement is built with five positional parameters: session identifier (rendered as a decimal string), agent UUID, message kind, content (which may be null), and auxiliary JSON data (which may be null). The statement requests the database to return the assigned message ID. The result is wrapped so that it is automatically released when the temporary context is freed.
behavior.insertion-success: When the database reports a successful command result, the operation returns success with no additional value. The temporary memory context and all allocations within it are released.
behavior.insertion-failure:
When the database reports any result other than a successful command, an error message is produced. The temporary memory context is released after the operation, so the error message must survive that release.
The error message is allocated on the database context rather than on the temporary context. Allocating it on the temporary context would cause it to be freed before the caller could read it, producing a dangling pointer. By attaching it to the longer-lived database context, the error string remains valid after the temporary context is destroyed.
behavior.tool-result-construction: A tool result message record is constructed in memory without being persisted. The record carries an ID of zero to signal that it has not yet been assigned a database identity. Its kind is fixed as "tool_result". The content field holds the provided output text verbatim. The auxiliary JSON data encodes four fields: the tool call identifier being responded to, the tool name, the raw output text, and a success boolean. The success flag is extracted by parsing the output text as JSON and reading the "tool_success" field; if that parse fails or the field is absent, success defaults to false.
